sentences.text: My name is Maurice Meyers.
And I live at 171 on 688th Avenue, in Flushing, Queens.
And my date of birth-- June 6, '08.
In Ostrowiec, Poland.
O-S-T-R-O-V-I-E-C.
No, my name at birth was Moshe Majerczyk.

But since the name is impossible to pronounce by American people, English, so when I applied for my citizenship papers, I changed my name to Meyers, because I had an uncle in Toronto by the name of Meyers.
Then I got the name.
And the judge congratulated me, said I did a fine thing, what I did.
Well--
Yes, before-- and before the war, my father had a nice retail and wholesale store-- textile, and hosiery, and haberdasher.
Chaim.
And after I finished my school, I-- he took me-- he was happy to take me in right away to the store because he was getting older and he needed some fresh blood in it.
Well af-- we had our school-- I would say our seven year school was like 10, 11, or 12 years in the United States high school.
And third grade, we had already geography, history, and nature, [POLISH], which is called.
In the fifth grade, we had already French languages.
So when-- when we finish-- after my seventh grade, I was prepared to go to gymnasium, which is like a lyceum, like a college.
And I was prepared for the examination.
And when my mother-- I went with my mother to the director of this gymnasium.
And which-- he was a friend of ours.
And he was a customer of our store.
So he said, Mrs.
Majerczyk, I have already three Jews in this school.
How can I take another Jew?
I-- you want me to lose my job?
And this was the end of my schooling.
However, I still went to Hebrew, to yeshiva.
And actually, when we started school at 8 o'clock till 1:00, after I came home and had lunch or dinner, I went back to cheder till 8 o'clock.
So my-- my youth was from 8:00 in the morning till 8:00 at night.
Only on the holidays I had time to fool around and to play soccer.
This was another school, which is called cheder, yeshiva, whatever you want to call it.
Non-Jewish.
We were maybe three, four kids in the class, Jewish kids.
Out of 35, 32.
Well small-- oh, sure.
We were happy when we went to school and didn't get beaten up.
Naturally, we were always getting beaten up.
In the school itself, I had this-- a fight with the son of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] teacher's son was with me.
And we had also a kind of a little anti-Semitic fight.
We had a teacher, a woman teacher.
And our geography-- instead of giving us geography, she gave us how not to let the mothers go into a Jewish store and to buy.
This was years, years ago.
I described it as anti-Semitism was, in Poland, born.
And they were living with it.
Between-- we had about eight, 10 teachers.
Some of them were liberal.
Some of them were nice.
And some of them were bloody anti-Semites, in school, in class.
You cannot-- I couldn't help myself.
It was the times before Christmas that, in front of my father's store, was standing a tall boy and showing people, don't go in.
This is a Jewish store.
We lived through these kinds-- these times also in Poland.
It wasn't all sweet.
Before the war, when I was young, my town had 35,000 people.
I would say about 5,000, 6,000 Jews.
Most of the time, yes.
It was always like a ghetto.
But we didn't live there.
We lived-- we were living outside rather from-- from the main ghetto city.
Number one, it was nicer to live there.
No, and I think this was the thing-- my father got an apartment there.
And we were living there.
I was brought up there.
It's called [POLISH].
And [POLISH], which was much nicer than in the ghetto, much nicer.
Well, I would say medium off.
Very well, I wouldn't say.
But we had always a nice pair of shoes to wear and a suit, and what to eat-- enough food to eat in the house.
And since my father had-- how many-- three sisters-- one, two, three, four-- so after he married off every-- every daughter, he got rid of the money.
Then he had to work for the next daughter.
Because in Europe, if you want to marry off a daughter, you have to give a nadn, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which each time he saved for the next one.
Oh my-- very traditional.
My father was very Orthodox.
And on his-- on his time, when he was not in business, he was sitting at the Talmud, day and night, whenever he had an hour, you could only see him at the Talmud, at the table with the study.
And I learned a lot from my father.
He did study with me, too.
And I know a lot of things, thanks to my father.
Oh, yes.
Close is not enough.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father was to me like God.
No, I was rather the-- after-- the youngest.
And after me was a younger daughter, a younger girl.
I was the second from the youngest, not from the oldest.
Oh, ultra Orthodox.
Friday night, we closed the store an hour be-- an hour before [YIDDISH], before kindling the lights.
And then actually, we went all-- I went with my father to the synagogue.
And we came home.
It was a traditional dinner at the table.
Every child was around, sitting at the table, with kiddish, with all the-- all the trimmings, 100%.
Now, since we didn't live in the ghetto, we didn't belong to the main ghetto synagogue, to the big Jewish synagogue.
But we had a friend and neighbor.
He had a big house.
So he donated his basement, a big basement hall, for the synagogue.
This synagogue was called a shtiebel in Yiddish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had about, I would say, 200 members, 200 families, members.
My father was everything there.
He was the originator.
He was the [HEBREW].
On the holidays, he davened Kol Nidre.
He davened [HEBREW], [HEBREW].
He blows the shofar.
He read the Torah.
He was everything.
He was everything.
But he wasn't an ordained rabbi.
No, no, he was a businessman.
This he did on the side, for no money.
This was no-- this only honorable job.
And since he was one of the most learned Jews in this neighborhood, so he was doing all these things.
Naturally, he had a sexton, which did the dirty work, you know?
But he did the-- all these main thing in the synagogue.
Oh yes.
He-- he never was running for the office or for-- I mean, for the city council.
Like, he didn't like these things.
But he was-- they wouldn't-- like, Saturday morning, they wouldn't start daven before my father came.
My father came, they start daven.
He was very honorable.
Well, the relationship with the Christian community was that they-- in the store, we had 95% of our customers were Christian.
We had a better line of merchandise.
We carried a better line.

And this-- the Polish intelligentsia, which means, let's say, the engineers from-- from the steel mills, which were quite a few thousand workers there, and teachers-- and this was the better class of people, were our customers.
And thanks to that, it also helped me a lot to get a job in the steel mill.
I wasn't a plain worker.
But I had-- I was a [POLISH], like I had 30 boys working under me, in the steel mill.
I was sitting in the office.
Well, we could make a easier living.
You could make a easier living.
That's-- that's what it is.
Oh, my mother was a lovely woman.
All-- we had all respect for our-- our parents got all the respect, not like today.
But whatever they said, it was done.
Naturally, she was a hardworking woman, because she was in the store, just with my father.
Friday, she took off to prepare for Shabbos, to cook, and bake, and make the challahs, and make the fish for Shabbos.
But--
But-- sure.
But otherwise, every day she was working in the store.
And she was very important in the store, because she spoke very nice Polish.
My father spoke Russian.
But my mother spoke nice Polish.
If I had-- oh, when I grew up?
Oh, sure.
Oh, sure.
Number one, in school-- we went to school with friends, all the years.
We became friends, and boys and girls too.
Sure, I remember.
You mean the names?
Sure.
Maybe [INAUDIBLE] close relationship.
The name was-- we had our next door neighbor, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was my-- downstairs, where we were living, was a boy by [NON-ENGLISH].
I went to school all the years with a boy by the name of [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH].
We had quite a few, quite a few names.
Saturday afternoon, when my father and mother went to sleep, after dinner, we had free from-- from like, 2 o'clock to 6, 7 o'clock.
Then we played.
Wintertime, we ice skate.
And naturally, it wasn't 100% kosher to take the steel-- metal skates, but I hide it under my coat.
And I went out of-- away from the city, when we could enjoy ourselves.
In summertime, we went also to play ball, soccer.
This was our-- and a couple of weeks, we had swimming, also.
When it was a Jewish holiday, like Tisha B'Av, or-- we went swimming, whenever we had free.
But a whole week, we were busy from 8:00 in the morning till 8:00 at night.
And then sometimes I worked on my lessons till 12:00, midnight.
And my father said, when are you going to sleep?
Cut off the light already.
It's late.
And sometimes I wasn't finished at 12:00 and 12:30 at night.
Were any of your friends Gentiles, Christians?
No.
Christian friends, no.
Oh, yes.
Since I was maybe 10 years old, I was in Zionist movement.
And later on, I was in Betar.
Matter of fact, I was [HEBREW].
The last two years, before the war, I was the head of Betar in my town, in Ostrowiec.
To tell you-- to tell you the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you-- what do you say there?
The idea of Betar.

When we started to be Zionists, and when Jabotinsky start his movement, that just halutzim in Israel, and the way how they are living, the kibbutzim, without a gun, and without-- they should be secure.
Without security, it's not worth nothing.
So he came and he-- and with the idea that the Jews in Israel have to have guns.
They called him a Hitlerite, that look at that.
The Jews are going against England, such a world power.
And they want the Jews should have guns in Israel?
So naturally, he was not loved by a lot of people.
But later on, the world saw that he was the biggest prophet in our life.
What do you remember from your experience in the group?
Like, what did you-- what was a typical Betar meeting for you and your friends?
Well, every Saturday we had meetings.
We couldn't have meetings during the week because we have schools and things like that.
But Saturday, we had meetings, speeches, and work hours.
You know, we're working, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] doing like saving money for Keren Kayemeth, and all these-- whatever we could do.
My father was a religious man and a Zionist.
A lot of religious Jews, before the war, were anti-Zionists.
A matter of fact, the [HEBREW] movement became Zionist after the war.
Before the war, they were anti and fighting the Zionists, because they were not religious enough.
They didn't eat kosher enough, or they didn't eat-- they didn't keep Sabbath.
My father did, oh yes.
We did.
And he was a Zionist-- always supported.
They came, these speakers, from big towns.
He went to the speeches.
He donate Keren Kayemeth.
There was Keren Hayesod.
He gave money for Keren Hayesod, Keren Kayemeth-- yes, supported.
From Warsaw.
They come--
Oh, yes.
I started to travel to Warsaw when I was 19 years.
My father was happy to send me.
He shouldn't travel.
It was tiresome for him.
And he gave me a bundle of money.
I put it in my-- on my body, a bundle of money.
And he gave me notes.
And naturally, I made a list which manufactures to visit.
And I knew-- I started to know, almost better than him, the colors and qualities and styling of-- we carried sweaters.
We carried men's shirts, very big neckties, socks.
So I understood-- I understood the-- this line very well.
And he was happy to see that I am doing well.
And thanks to this, I did it till-- till today, till I came to the United States.
Oh, sure.
Number one, we had a Polish paper, which is called Nasz Przeglad.
This was a beautiful Zionist paper-- very, very high level, like the Israeli-- Israel has an English paper, the Jerusalem Post.
This is the type of paper.
We had the Polish, naturally.
Naturally, we took interest in the Sejm senate, which is congress and-- congress and senate, which we had in Poland.
We called Sejm.
We had-- we saw to it we should have Zionist members, congressmen, which was a very famous Yitzhak Gruenbaum, one of the famous Zionist congress.
Naturally, the ultra religious had also their people in congress and in senate.
They had a senator also, a Jewish senator.
Very few, but we had some representation.
Oh, sure we followed-- we followed the events in Germany.
We had already radio, before the war.
This was the last years before the war, radio came to Poland.
And we had newspaper, Jewish newspapers, which my father had every day.
It's called the Haynt, a Zionist newspaper.
Very few houses had it, because it cost some 20 or 25 grosze, which was a few pennies.
Not everybody can.
And so when you're-- when one family read it, the next neighbor came to borrow it, to read it next day.
This how we-- this is how it was.
Rather not.
I read rather not the Jewish only.
I read the Polish newspapers-- Polish.
It meant a lot to us, because when Hitler came to power, in 1933, we started to worry about it.
A matter of fact, in 1935, Jabotinsky, in his speech, said, please, Jews.
Leave Poland.
You are sitting on fire now.
Get out of here.
But the Jews-- these which were well up didn't listen to him.
And there wasn't really where to go.
Go-- but there wasn't where to go.
The masses didn't have money to move, even for a ticket to go.
And there was no-- every door was closed.
Before the war, no.
Why?
I will tell you why.
Because we were comfortable.
And so we didn't think about going.
I had an uncle, my mother's brother from Staszow, which he didn't make a living.
He grew up in this very small town.
So he went to the United States.
And he lived through the war here in the United States.
This was the only uncle I had.
I had also, in 1938, was invited my mother's cousin, Dr.
Anna Goldfeber, by Roosevelt was invite this, because she was a cancer scientist.
And in those days, cancer science in the United States was on zero level-- level.
And she was one of the first-- one of the first.
Because the first was the [?
Strenk ?] Cancer Institute.
And she was the next, which she started and worked in Columbia.
And then she worked in NYU.
The last 15, 20 years of her life she finished in NYU, had her laboratory with her mice, with her doctors' work.
That's the trouble.
That-- that's the $64,000 question.
We were in Poland, but we never, never felt that we are citizens.
We were citizens.
We went-- when-- they had the [POLISH] there for a president, for a senate, we went.
We had the right to vote.
But we were never felt that we are citizen, that I can love this country-- the opposite from the United States.
Here, I feel that I am a citizen.
I love this country.
And I would give my life for it.
But not in Poland, not at all.
Naturally.
No, we didn't have a sense of be-- we-- when it was quiet life, no pogroms, we were happy.
When-- when they didn't put in a guy in front of my father's store, don't buy from a Jew.
This a Jew's store.
When it was quiet, we were happy.
But from times to times, you always had these excesses, which happened.
But we didn't feel trapped.
No, we didn't feel trapped.
But we didn't think of moving or emigrating.
We didn't think about it.
